Transcrição Completa: 4 Attacking Business Bottlenecks w Zach
[00:00] Appreciate it. Appreciate it. So, um yeah, today we are going to talk about uh attacking business bottlenecks. Um specifically your agency's bottlenecks.
[00:12] I I think at least I can speak for myself. I cannot speak for all of you guys, but if you guys are dealing with clients, it's so easy to get caught up in trying to solve bottlenecks for them and completely neglecting the health of your own business and uh not even treating your business like a clients.
[00:28] Like sometimes you guys prioritize your clients way over your own business. And uh I I think that can oftentimes hinder growth. So we're going to talk a little bit about that.
[00:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Attacking Business Bottlenecks - Key Pivots I've Made to Grow to Seven Figures"
NÚMEROS: Q1 2023: $20-30K/mês → Atual: $100,000+/mês MRR
VISUAL: Slide de título com comparação temporal, indicadores de crescimento, transição de estado anterior para atual
[00:45] So uh we're going to talk uh I don't know subtitle here is key pivots I've made to grow to seven figures. To be clear, last year I did not do seven figures. We are absolutely pacing far beyond seven figures this year.
[01:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q1 2023 vs Now"
NÚMEROS:
Q1 2023: $20-30K/mês
Agora: $100,000+/mês MRR
2 semanas em Dubai sem trabalhar
6 dias em Cabo sem trabalhar
VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado mostrando estado anterior (preso, sem leads, zero tempo livre) vs estado atual (pipeline cheio, não envolvido em trabalho com clientes, viagens)
[01:15] Um for a little example here, you can see in Q1 of 2023 I was stuck between 20 to 30K per month. I did not have a regular stream of leads or booked calls. I also had like zero free time to work on growing the business.
[01:30] Um, right now we are doing over $100,000 a month in MRR. Uh, we have a full pipeline of qualified leads. Uh, and we at like I'm hardly involved in client work right now. Like actually hardly involved.
[01:45] I just spent two weeks in Dubai. Did not touch my computer once, did not talk to a client once, did not talk to my team once. They uh in that time they actually onboarded a new client, completely got them up and running, started getting them qualified leads and booked appointments. Didn't didn't touch it at all.
[02:05] Came back for 3 days and I went to Cabo for six days. once again did not touch my computer once. Our clients are thriving right now and um it is it is a night and day difference from where we were a year ago.
[02:20] So, we're going to talk about kind of the main p um I'd say the main pivots that we have made as an agency and the main bottlenecks we had to solve to get to where we are here and hopefully you guys can take some of the core lessons.
[02:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What is a Bottleneck?"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum específico
VISUAL: Definição destacada, conceito de fluxo de energia no negócio, representação visual de bloqueio/atraso em processo ou sistema
[02:40] It's not necessarily implementing the exact same things but uh I want you guys to analyze the thought process and the way in which we have gone about solving these bottlenecks cuz uh if you guys can think outside of the box a little bit I think there can be some awesome takeaways for each and every one of you. So let's get started.
[02:58] All right. What is a bottleneck? Um just the definition alone I think is actually helpful to uh think about. It is a situation that causes delay in a process or a system.
[03:10] Business like I'm all about like in the most nonwo way ever. business is just about like energy flow. Like if you have really bad energy yourself, like uh we were talking about like um biology earlier today with Jonathan.
[03:25] It's like if you're not sleeping well, you're not eating right, like you're not going to have the right energy to put into your business in and of itself, but also the business itself needs to be taken care of to make sure that energy can continue to flow through it and uh momentum can continue to be had and maintained.
[03:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "4 Core Areas Where Bottlenecks Occur"
NÚMEROS: 4 áreas principais
VISUAL: Lista vertical ou grid com 4 caixas representando: 1) Leads, 2) Lead Nurturing, 3) Sales/Conversion, 4) Fulfillment & Operations. Ícones correspondentes para cada área
[03:50] Now, um the four core areas that I feel like and I might be missing one like I actually thought about putting financial management uh on here, but I would say the four primary areas in business where bottlenecks may be occurring in your business right now would be leads.
[04:08] So, getting new leads in general and number two, lead nurturing. So, making sure that those people uh not are just expressing interest in working with you or giving you their information, but actually being able to get to the point where they trust you enough that they actually care to do business with you.
[04:25] So once again, leads, lead nurture, sales or the conversion mechanism. Maybe that's a sales call, maybe that's uh like the live events that I was talking about yesterday or webinars like whatever the conversion mechanism is that might need a little bit of work.
[04:40] And then lastly, actual fulfillment and operations because uh it's pretty easy to go get a client and if you're not treating them properly or maybe you guys are offering a less than stellar service, um you guys can very easily lose those clients if you're not fulfilling and operating properly.
[04:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Road Less Stupid Framework"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum específico
VISUAL: Framework de perguntas, citação do livro de Keith Cunningham, estrutura de questionamento para resolver problemas de negócio
[05:05] So uh a good question that I try to ask and I learned this from a book called The Road Less Stupid by Keith Cunningham. Um it is just a the most simple framework for asking the question, but that entire book real quick if anyone's never read that.
[05:20] It's not really something that I suggest, hey go read cover to cover. It's more so a great resource to go into when you're dealing with something in your business. The entire book is based on or it's it's it's all on um giving you questions to ask for your business about solving certain problems that you may be incurring.
[05:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Problem-Solving Framework: What's the best way to achieve XYZ result WHILE or WITHOUT a certain constraint?"
NÚMEROS: Exemplo: menos de 5 horas/semana de treino
VISUAL: Template de pergunta destacado, exemplo de fitness usado como analogia, estrutura de pensamento direcionado com restrições
[05:45] But one of the easiest frameworks here is what's the best way to achieve XY result or XYZ result while or without a certain constraint. So for example, let's say you're trying to get fit and you have like no time.
[06:00] It's not like what's the best way to get fit because I could tell you go work out 10 hours a day and I don't think Jonathan would appreciate that advice but um in instead you might say what's the best way to work out or I'm sorry what's the best way to get fit while working out less than five hours total per week.
[06:18] It's it's going to direct your problem solving in a certain direction. So for me whenever I was trying to solve the issue going back to once again I was doing 20 to 30k per month and I had like no time.
[06:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "My Question: How do I get an endless supply of quality leads WITHOUT doing manual outbound or hiring VAs or salespeople?"
NÚMEROS: $20-30K/mês (estado anterior)
VISUAL: Pergunta específica destacada, restrições claramente definidas (sem outbound manual, sem VAs, sem vendedores), foco em automação
[06:35] I don't know why I was so caught up in the business. I was in Slack a ton. Uh more than I I I would like to admit. Um I wasn't even in Facebook Ads Manager all that much, but it was just a lot of client work and just dealing with random little headaches that were coming up with clients.
[06:50] So for me, I said, "How do I get an endless supply of quality leads without doing manual outbound or hiring VAS or sales people?" Because I really I didn't have a whole lot of people I could manage. Like I didn't have the time to manage them properly.
[07:08] Um, and I really didn't have the time myself to go do a whole bunch of manual outbound at the time. I had had previous success with doing, um, the cold video pitches like Jeremy talks about. It's just timeconuming.
[07:22] And so, uh, the answer for me, I said, well, let's practice what I preach and run some ads. Um, that's what I was doing for the majority of my clients. Why wouldn't I do the same?
[07:35] Now, quick quick question here. if I had ads and marketing clients. Uh I had a client roster and they were all leveraging ads and like uh sales funnels and stuff, but ultimately they're all in different verticals and they're all in different sales processes um and really kind of slightly different nuanced work from client to client to client.
[07:55] Do you guys think that I would have a successful time achieving clients just by going and turning on some ads saying, "Hey, I'm a marketer. I can help you scale up your your sales." No. Why not?
[08:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Bottlenecks Behind the Bottlenecks"
NÚMEROS: 3 problemas identificados
VISUAL: Camadas de problemas revelados, conceito de profundidade de análise, lista de obstáculos ocultos
[08:15] Okay. Yeah. Yeah. So, actually guys, when you're trying to solve bottlenecks, I have found that there's often times bottlenecks behind the bottlenecks. There's usually the surface level problem and then there's more there there's there's uh an extra layer of depth behind that.
[08:32] Similar to what uh Jonathan was talking about earlier. He's like, "Why is this important to you?" And you say xyz. It's like no actually be real with yourself. Like, why is that truly important? there's usually further things that you have to go into.
[08:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "3 Hidden Problems Discovered"
NÚMEROS:

Sem mensagem específica
Sem SOPs/sistemas
VISUAL: Lista numerada de problemas fundamentais, cada um bloqueando a solução superficial dos anúncios, representação de análise profunda
[08:55] And so, uh, for me, yeah, someone needs to turn their their stuff on mute. But, uh, anyway, for me, I realized that that would not work because ultimately, I had no time to take on more clients. Even if the ads worked, I couldn't take on more clients. Like, I just didn't have the time to service them.
[09:15] Secondly, I had no specific result or messaging that would actually attract new cold clients. It would be like a real estate agent saying, "Hey, uh, I'm Jimmy. Uh, I can help you guys sell or buy homes. You want to do business with me? No. We all know like five five to 10 real estate agents. Why in the world would we give our our our business to Jimmy? It's not specific enough. It's not unique.
[09:40] So, I knew that what I had at the moment would not work to a cold market. And then thirdly, I had no SOPs in place to systemize the work. So, even if I was to go hire an employee, which I thought about and actually I was in the process of doing, I realized I'm going to set my employee up for total chaos if I have no true systems for them to plug into.
[10:02] they're just going to get tapped out on time just as quick as I will and it's really going to be extremely inefficient on my time to try to train that employee.
[10:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Solution: Productized Service"
NÚMEROS: 2 pontos de conversão
VISUAL: Estrutura de serviço produtizado, sistema plug-and-play, representação de processo escalável e replicável
[10:15] So, the answer I came up with was the first real solution I said I need to create a productized service that I could create systems around uh like like uh SOPs around for an employee to easily plug into so that way I could free up my time in client facing work.
[10:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Call Booking Funnel System"
NÚMEROS: 2 pontos de conversão
VISUAL: Fluxograma do sistema: Anúncios → Facebook Lead Form → 2 caminhos de conversão (Mini Webinar + AI Bot), processo visual de funil completo
[10:40] And what that looked like is uh the actual service itself was uh simple call booking funnels. Okay, so running ads to a Facebook lead form with qualifying questions and then having two points of converting them to book calls.
[10:55] So have a mini webinar which Jeremy talks about. Literally just use his framework. Nothing crazy there. It works very very well. No need to recreate the wheel. And then also added on an AI appointment booking bot. Uh I personally use Woo Sender. Uh it's pretty pretty great. It works really really well.
[11:15] And so I have two different points of conversion to get those leads to book calls. And then obviously it's up to the client sales team to be able to close those calls.
[11:25] So that was the service that I was going to be able to take to the market. I can create SOPs around all of that. It's really really easy to tell someone how to go write some basic ad copy. Really easy to tell someone to um create Facebook lead forms and kind of the methodologies of which you want to ask certain qualifying questions.
[11:45] Uh uh I actually had no idea how to use Wooender. So, I bought Wooender and then there's a training portal that they actually offered and I hired an employee and that was the first thing I had him do. I was like, "Hey, you got to go learn this because I don't even know how to operate it."
[12:02] So, you're going to be our Woo Sendender guy. And uh and then um yeah, it was up to me to help uh our clients write the mini webinar. So, I understood the systems. Now, I needed the offer.

[14:38] Now, qualified leads, we had a qualifying question on there that said, "Are you doing at least seven figures per year in your service-based business?" Yes or no slash please do not proceed. So, the people that said yes.
[14:55] Yo, turn your stuff on Milton mute. You're muted, dude. All right. Um so we had 754 leads but of those 431 selfmarked themselves saying yes I am doing at least seven figures per uh per year in my service based business.
[15:15] Um what that spit out we had four closed deals at a cost of acquisition of 5K. That's pretty inefficient just to be clear. Uh however we did spend 20k and overall between the clients that we had collected there we did make $49,000 at the time that I was collecting all this data.
[15:35] So, uh, we were profitable, but like just really inefficient. We're booking a lot of calls. Our average cost per qualified sales call that we weren't cancelling was $114. Wasn't upset with that. There were some downfalls of that. But overall, I was like, the system kind of works, but like I I I've got to turn this off.

[16:35] Uh, and and to be clear, like I I was not actually the one doing sales. I quickly quickly hired three different salespeople. And um I I just did not set them up for success. I hired them too quickly. I did not have a proper onboarding and interview process.
[16:52] Uh I did not have any training resources to give them. I was not monitoring their KPIs near as uh as specific as I should have been.

NÚMEROS: Nenhum específico

[17:30] I realized uh ultimately what what this all left me feeling was I felt incredibly frustrated that the success of our business was entirely reliant on actions that were not directly in our control.
[17:45] If a client was not doing what they needed to do, we would not be able to retain business and we cannot scale to the degree that I want to scale to.
[17:55] Um, no great business I have never seen a great business that is their entire business model is reliant upon something completely outside of their control.

And we want to figure out how do we make our clients extremely successful at sales without having to say, "Hey client, great. We're getting you great results. However, you guys suck at sales. So now you have to go spend another $10,000 on a sales training program.
[20:08] Why not be able to give them access to our own program in due time when it's ready and when we feel like we are actually capable of it. But why not have our own program that we could give to a client the very second they onboard.
[20:22] So that way from the moment they sign that contract, their sales team can start training. So that way, let's call it 2 3 weeks later whenever we have everything ready to go, we press publish, they're going to be a little bit more likely to be able to have success with these leads that we're helping get them.

[21:40] Uh we've also implemented an incredible referral system. Feel free to use this if you guys would like. Uh I reached out to all of her clients in December and uh I I sent them fun little selfie videos.
[21:55] I was like, "Hey James, hey Natalya. Uh guys, heads up. I actually don't want your money next month. Uh, what I want instead is I want you to give me a referral and if that referral closes, uh, I will wave the next month's fee that you guys would pay us, including any revshares that you would pay us as well.
[22:15] For some of our clients, that's like 20 to $30,000 that they could save in that next month's profit. And so anyway, uh, when we did that, we immediately started getting way more qualified referrals from our clients. You know, believe it or not, they like their profit.
[22:32] So, uh, anyway, started doing that. That started working. Um, and have already closed deals from client referrals on that.
[22:40] Uh we are also uh once again like I mentioned I promoted my marketing guide to client success director uh which has completely taken me out of almost all client communication. I spend zero time in Slack for the most part.
[22:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Woo Sender Details & ROI"
NÚMEROS:

VISUAL: Breakdown de custos do Woo Sender, estrutura de preços detalhada, ROI do sistema de AI booking
[22:58] Um we are also once again using Woo Center so that way we're not solely reliant on um on sales teams to be calling up leads which we do encourage but we just can't rely on them to do that. We're also not solely reliant on VSSLs to convert people into booked calls.
[23:15] So, uh, we found that on average every single lead form fill that goes through Facebook, roughly 20% at least of those people will actually book a call through Woo Sender.
[23:28] Woo Sender, by the way, what it is, uh, whenever they fill out their information, Woo Sender will immediately text them and it'll be like, "Hey, Jimmy, just saw that you were uh, just saw that you submitted your info to learn more about our XYZ service. Would this time, this time, or this time be good for a quick call with you?"
[23:48] And all you do is you hook up your Google calendar into Woo Sender and it will pull your your calendar availability to then just give a couple immediate recommendations for times that that prospect can connect with you. You hook up your entire sales team in there. It's really really seamless.
[24:08] Fully transparent. If you guys would like to look into it, it is $5,000 onboarding fee. I I have no I have no uh benefit in telling you guys this. Just letting you guys know. It's a $5,000 onboarding fee with them. It's $2,000 per month base retainer and then $500 per month in additional clients that you bring onto your Woo Center account.
[24:32] So, there is a hard cost. However, it's it's nominal for the amount of results that uh I feel it can uh it can help you get.
[24:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Client Gifting System"

[25:02] She was finding them on Facebook, uh Instagram, etc. she's trying to find their birthdays and then obviously Christmas is just one one simple date. But uh we started giving all of our clients uh gifts as well. Uh spending anywhere between $100 and $200 per gift on on these gifts. Like we're not cheaping out.
[25:22] Um I mean I guess you could get way more expensive than that. But anyway, our clients are super super appreciative of those surprise uh gifts.
[25:30] And then I've also joined another mastermind uh which just obviously continuing to level up and uh uh I'll stay in here for forever hopefully. But um anyway, just another way to continue networking.
[25:45] I've also gotten some clients from other masterminds when, believe it or not, when you're not surrounding yourself with just marketing people, it's pretty easy to get more clients whenever you're just uh networking and showing them what you do.
[26:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Motivation Tip: Dubai Experience"
NÚMEROS:
$14,000 (voo business class de volta)
$3,000 (voo economy de volta)
15 horas (duração do voo)
VISUAL: Comparação humorística entre business class e economy, lição sobre motivação através de experiências, contraste situacional
[26:02] And then uh also, if you guys ever just want to simply be motivated to make a whole lot more money, um go to Dubai. That's a helpful way to go. And then uh if you guys really want to be motivated to make more money, use points to book business class to go to Dubai.
[26:20] and then do it when they have uh blackout dates uh on the return trip and you can't use points and then you're like crap I got I got to either pay $14,000 for a return trip uh or I can pay 3,000 $3,000 and be an economy on that return trip for 15 hours.
[26:40] And uh Choy uh didn't think that economy was going to be as bad as it was. And so I said, "Yeah, we'll just book we'll book economy on the way back." So I set the baseline.
[26:52] We're talking about situational um who was that yesterday that was talking about those three criterias for um for leveling up. And one of them I think it was Jeremy. He was talking about like Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. And one of those was situations and like material items.
[27:10] It was like I set my baseline on the way out there with a sick business class. And then on the way back I had 15 hours of handlebars just being jammed in my knees. Couldn't sleep at all. Like man I need to make so much more money so I don't care about a $14,000 flight.
[27:28] Um anyway, those are some just random little ideas. If you guys want to take from those, feel free.
[27:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Key Takeaways: 3 Decision-Making Benchmarks"
NÚMEROS: 3 critérios principais
VISUAL: Framework de tomada de decisão destacado, três pilares para todas as decisões de negócio
[27:38] But o ultimately the key the key things I want you guys to take away here is relentlessly attack bottlenecks in your business. It doesn't matter if it's client acquisition, uh lead nurture, making sure that people are efficiently uh getting used to you guys and trusting you.
[27:55] Uh whether it's getting clients better results and creating systems around that, relentlessly attack those bottlenecks and do so in a way that will number one create revenue, do it profitably, and three sustainably.
[28:10] Those are the three benchmarks that I make all of my decisions based on for all of our clients as well as our organization. If we're going to do something, it's got to be revenue driven. It can't just do revenue. It's also got to do so profitably.
[28:28] And then thirdly, I don't care to go send some email out that's going to make a whole bunch of money. If we can't repurpose that and continue to create a system around it to uh to ultimately just continue profiting from it, we're not going to do it. So,
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
Q1 2023: $20-30K/mês → Atual: $100,000+/mês MRR
Crescimento: 300-400% em receita mensal
Teste de anúncios: $20,000 investidos (Junho-Outubro)
754 leads totais gerados
431 leads qualificados (fazem $1M+/ano)
175 chamadas de vendas agendadas
4 negócios fechados
CAC: $5,000
CPL médio: $26
Custo por chamada qualificada: $114
Receita gerada no teste: $49,000
Taxa de conversão vendas: ~2.3% (175 calls → 4 fechamentos)
Woo Sender conversão: 20% de leads para chamadas
2 semanas Dubai + 6 dias Cabo sem trabalhar
Investimento em consultor operações: $40,000
Sistema presentes clientes: $100-200/presente
Economia referral: $20,000-30,000 por cliente
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Serviço Produtizado - Call Booking Funnels:
Anúncios → Facebook Lead Form com perguntas qualificadoras
2 pontos de conversão: Mini Webinar (framework Jeremy) + AI Bot (Woo Sender)
Sistema totalmente documentado em SOPs
Woo Sender (AI Appointment Bot):
Custo: $5K onboarding + $2K/mês base + $500/mês por cliente adicional
Texta leads automaticamente oferecendo horários
Integração com Google Calendar
Conversão: 20% de leads para chamadas agendadas
Sistema de Referência:
Oferta: Próximo mês grátis (incluindo revshare) por referência fechada
Vídeos selfie personalizados para clientes
Economia cliente: $20-30K potencial
Sistema de Presentes para Clientes:
Pesquisa em redes sociais (Facebook, Instagram)
Rastreamento de aniversários
Investimento: $100-200 por presente
Treinamento de Vendas:
Programas: Jeremy Lee Miner, Jordan Stupar, Jeremy Haynes
Call reviews com clientes
Desenvolvimento de programa próprio para 2024
Gestão Operacional:
SOPs específicos no ClickUp
Consultor de operações: $40K investimento
Promoção de funcionário a Director of Operations
Zero tempo em Slack
ESTRUTURA:
Framework de Resolução de Problemas: "Qual a melhor forma de alcançar XYZ resultado ENQUANTO ou SEM determinada restrição?"
4 Áreas Principais de Bottlenecks:
Leads (geração)
Lead Nurturing (nutrição/confiança)
Sales/Conversion (mecanismo de conversão)
Fulfillment & Operations (entrega e operações)
3 Benchmarks para Todas as Decisões:
Criar Receita
Fazer com Lucro
De Forma Sustentável (sistemática/replicável)
Conceito "Bottlenecks Behind Bottlenecks":
Problema superficial vs problema raiz
Análise em camadas de profundidade
Exemplo: Precisava de leads → Na verdade precisava de oferta específica + sistemas + tempo
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Investimento em Ads: $20,000
ROI Teste: $49,000 receita / $20,000 investido = 2.45x (mas ineficiente)
CAC: $5,000 (muito alto)
CPL: $26 (aceitável)
Custo/Chamada Qualificada: $114 (bom)
MRR Atual: $100,000+
Crescimento Anual: 7 figuras+ (pacing)
Custos Operacionais Adicionados:
Consultor: $40,000 (one-time)
Woo Sender: $5K setup + $2K-2.5K/mês
Presentes: ~$1,200-2,400/ano (12 clientes)
Treinamento vendas: Múltiplos programas
Resultado Líquido: Sistema lucrativo mas inicialmente ineficiente em vendas (2.3% conversão). Solução: Investir massivamente em treinamento de vendas e criar sistemas de suporte para clientes.
DURAÇÃO: ~28 minutos FORMATO: Apresentação educacional/case study para agências de marketing
